content:
# 🚨 MANDATORY: Team Memory System 🚨

A shared team memory stores critical gotchas, conventions, and debugging insights across sessions.

## REQUIRED: /recall BEFORE Starting Any Task

You MUST run /recall at the start of every task to check for relevant prior knowledge. Skipping this risks repeating known mistakes and wasting time on already-solved problems.

```
/recall "<topic or keyword relevant to your task>"
```

Examples:
- Working on Stripe integration? → /recall "Stripe"
- Fixing a database query? → /recall "database" or /recall "Drizzle"
- Debugging a test? → /recall "testing patterns"
- Working on team creation? → /recall "team" or /recall "createId"
- Touching waivers? → /recall "waiver"

**Do NOT skip this step.** Past sessions have stored critical gotchas that will save significant debugging time.

## REQUIRED: /remember After Discoveries

When you discover something useful — a gotcha, root cause, convention, or debugging insight — you MUST save it immediately with /remember. Do not wait until the end of the session.

```
/remember "<what you learned>" --category=gotcha|convention|debugging|architecture|workflow --priority=critical|moderate|ephemeral
```

## Summary

- **BEFORE work**: /recall to load relevant memories (MANDATORY)
- **DURING work**: /remember as soon as you discover something worth saving (MANDATORY)
- Failure to use team memory means the team loses knowledge and repeats mistakes.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- project-manager-linear: Use this agent when you need to manage project tasks using the Linear MCP. This includes creating new issues, editing issues, ensuring issues follow proper format and guidelines, breaking down large tasks into atomic units, and maintaining the project's Linear workflow. Examples: <example>Context: User wants to create a new issue for adding a feature. user: "I need to add a new authentication system to the project" assistant: "I'll use the project-manager-linear agent that will use Linear MCP to create a properly structured issue for this feature." <commentary>Since the user needs to create an issue for the project, use the Task tool to launch the project-manager-linear agent to ensure the issue follows Linear best practices.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User has multiple related features to implement. user: "We need to implement user profiles, settings page, and notification preferences" assistant: "Let me use the project-manager-linear agent to break these down into atomic, independent issues." <commentary>The user has a complex set of features that need to be broken down into proper atomic issues following Linear structure.</commentary></example> <example>Context: User wants to review if their issue description is properly formatted. user: "Can you check if this issue follows our guidelines: 'Implement user login'" assistant: "I'll use the project-manager-linear agent to review this issue against our Linear standards." <commentary>The user needs issue review, so use the project-manager-linear agent to ensure compliance with Linear guidelines.</commentary></example> (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)
- swarm:background-worker: Runs background-only tasks without MCP tool access. (Tools: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep, Bash)
- swarm:coordinator: Orchestrates swarm coordination and supervises worker agents. (Tools: All tools)
- swarm:worker: Executes a single subtask with file reservations and progress reporting. (Tools: All tools)

## claude.ai Linear
When passing string values to tools, send the content directly without escape sequences. For example, use real newlines in markdown content rather than literal backslash-n (\n) characters.

## deepwiki
DeepWiki MCP provides AI-powered documentation for GitHub repositories.

Available tools:
- read_wiki_structure: Get a list of documentation topics for a repository
- read_wiki_contents: View full documentation about a repository
- ask_question: Ask any question about a repository and get an AI-powered answer
- list_available_repos: List your available repositories (private mode only)
- generate_wiki: Generate a codebase wiki for a repository — only use when explicitly requested by the user (private mode only)
- devin_automation_manage: Manage Devin automations — list, get, create, update, delete, or fetch the trigger event schemas (private mode only)
- devin_knowledge_manage: Manage Devin knowledge notes and suggestions — list, search, get, create, update, delete notes, view folder structure, list/view/dismiss knowledge suggestions (private mode only)
- devin_playbook_manage: Manage Devin playbooks — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_schedule_manage: Manage scheduled Devin sessions — list, get, create, update, delete (private mode only)
- devin_session_create: Create one or more child Devin sessions (private mode only)
- devin_session_interact: Manage a Devin session — get status, send messages, sleep/terminate/archive, read messages & attachments, manage tags (private mode only)
- devin_session_events: Inspect session events — list summaries, fetch full details, or search event contents (private mode only)
- devin_session_search: Search and filter Devin sessions (private mode only)
- list_integrations: List all native integrations and MCP servers with their status and settings URLs (private mode only)

- adr-skill: Create and maintain Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) optimized for agentic coding workflows. Use when you need to propose, write, update, accept/reject, deprecate, or supersede an ADR; bootstrap an adr folder and index; consult existing ADRs before implementing changes; or enforce ADR conventions. This skill uses Socratic questioning to capture intent before drafting, and validates output against an agent-readiness checklist.
- agent-browser: Browser automation CLI for AI agents. Use when the user needs to interact with websites, including navigating pages, filling forms, clicking buttons, taking screenshots, extracting data, testing web apps, or automating any browser task. Triggers include requests to "open a website", "fill out a form", "click a button", "take a screenshot", "scrape data from a page", "test this web app", "login to a site", "automate browser actions", or any task requiring programmatic web interaction.
- breadboard-reflection: Breadboard Analysis
- breadboarding: Transform a workflow description into affordance tables showing UI and Code affordances with their wiring. Use to map existing systems or design new ones from shaped parts.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications (examples include websites, landing pages, dashboards, React components, HTML/CSS layouts, or when styling/beautifying any web UI). Generates creative, polished code and UI design that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- gitnexus-cli: Use when the user needs to run GitNexus CLI commands like analyze/index a repo, check status, clean the index, generate a wiki, or list indexed repos. Examples: "Index this repo", "Reanalyze the codebase", "Generate a wiki"
- gitnexus-debugging: Use when the user is debugging a bug, tracing an error, or asking why something fails. Examples: "Why is X failing?", "Where does this error come from?", "Trace this bug"
- gitnexus-exploring: Use when the user asks how code works, wants to understand architecture, trace execution flows, or explore unfamiliar parts of the codebase. Examples: "How does X work?", "What calls this function?", "Show me the auth flow"
- gitnexus-guide: Use when the user asks about GitNexus itself — available tools, how to query the knowledge graph, MCP resources, graph schema, or workflow reference. Examples: "What GitNexus tools are available?", "How do I use GitNexus?"
- gitnexus-impact-analysis: Use when the user wants to know what will break if they change something, or needs safety analysis before editing code. Examples: "Is it safe to change X?", "What depends on this?", "What will break?"
- gitnexus-pr-review: Use when the user wants to review a pull request, understand what a PR changes, assess risk of merging, or check for missing test coverage. Examples: "Review this PR", "What does PR #42 change?", "Is this PR safe to merge?"
- gitnexus-refactoring: Use when the user wants to rename, extract, split, move, or restructure code safely. Examples: "Rename this function", "Extract this into a module", "Refactor this class", "Move this to a separate file"
- impeccable: Use when the user wants to design, redesign, shape, critique, audit, polish, clarify, distill, harden, optimize, adapt, animate, colorize, extract, or otherwise improve a frontend interface. Covers websites, landing pages, dashboards, product UI, app shells, components, forms, settings, onboarding, and empty states. Handles UX review, visual hierarchy, information architecture, cognitive load, accessibility, performance, responsive behavior, theming, anti-patterns, typography, fonts, spacing, layout, alignment, color, motion, micro-interactions, UX copy, error states, edge cases, i18n, and reusable design systems or tokens. Also use for bland designs that need to become bolder or more delightful, loud designs that should become quieter, live browser iteration on UI elements, or ambitious visual effects that should feel technically extraordinary. Not for backend-only or non-UI tasks.
- linear: Manage issues, projects & team workflows in Linear. Use when the user wants to read, create or updates tickets in Linear.
- remove-ai-code-slop: Review a branch diff against main and remove AI-generated code slop. Use when asked to clean up AI code, review diffs for unnatural comments, defensive checks, unnecessary try/catch blocks, any casts, or inconsistent style before merging.
- repo-autopsy: Clone a GitHub repo into a local cache and perform deep analysis using full local toolchain (rg, ast-grep, git, fd, tokei, gitleaks). Use when the user wants to inspect, explore, audit, or understand an external GitHub repository — structure, dependencies, hotspots, exports, secrets, code stats, git blame, or targeted file reads. Triggers on phrases like "autopsy", "analyze this repo", "clone and explore", "what's inside owner/repo", or any request to investigate a third-party GitHub project without adding it to the current working tree.
- self-improving-agent: A universal self-improving agent that learns from ALL skill experiences. Uses multi-memory architecture (semantic + episodic + working) to continuously evolve the codebase. Auto-triggers on skill completion/error with hooks-based self-correction.
- seo-aeo-best-practices: SEO and AEO best practices for metadata, Open Graph, sitemaps, robots.txt, hreflang, JSON-LD structured data, EEAT, and content optimized for search engines and AI answer surfaces. Use this skill when implementing page SEO, technical SEO, schema markup, international SEO, AI-overview readiness, or improving content for Google, ChatGPT, Perplexity, and similar assistants.
- shaping: Use this methodology when collaboratively shaping a solution with the user - iterating on problem definition (requirements) and solution options (shapes).
- stripe-best-practices: Best practices for building Stripe integrations
- vercel-composition-patterns: React composition patterns that scale. Use when refactoring components with boolean prop proliferation, building flexible component libraries, or designing reusable APIs. Triggers on tasks involving compound components, render props, context providers, or component architecture. Includes React 19 API changes.
- vercel-react-best-practices: React and Next.js performance optimization guidelines from Vercel Engineering. This skill should be used when writing, reviewing, or refactoring React/Next.js code to ensure optimal performance patterns. Triggers on tasks involving React components, Next.js pages, data fetching, bundle optimization, or performance improvements.
- web-design-guidelines: Review UI code for Web Interface Guidelines compliance. Use when asked to "review my UI", "check accessibility", "audit design", "review UX", or "check my site against best practices".
- wzrrd-publish: Publish static files or directories to wzrrd.sh. Use for sharing agent-built pages, demos, receipts, docs, diagrams, or static artifacts at https://<slug>.wzrrd.sh/.
- alchemy-cloudflare: Alchemy IaC patterns for deploying TanStack Start apps to Cloudflare Workers with PlanetScale databases via Hyperdrive. Use when setting up new TanStack Start projects, configuring Alchemy deployments, working with PlanetScale/Drizzle migrations, local development with Cloudflare bindings, or deploying to custom domains.
- athlete-docs: Documentation guidance for competition athletes and volunteers in WODsmith. Use when writing, reviewing, or improving athlete-facing documentation including registration, scheduling, workout viewing, leaderboards, check-in, and volunteer coordination.
- claim-url-lookup: Look up claim URLs for placeholder users created via manual registration. Use when needing to find the sign-up claim link for a specific email address, debug claim token issues, or verify claim tokens exist in local KV. Triggers on "claim URL", "claim token", "claim link", "placeholder user sign-up link", or requests to find the registration invite link for an email.
- competition-organizer-docs: Generate and review documentation for competition organizers using Diataxis framework. Use when writing docs for organizer workflows like creating competitions, managing divisions, scheduling heats, handling registrations, entering results, or managing volunteers/judges.
- date-timezone: Handle date and timezone conversions correctly in forms and displays. Use when working with: Date inputs, new Date(), formatDate, toLocaleDateString, getDate(), getMonth(), getFullYear(), date parsing, UTC conversion, timezone issues, dates displaying wrong day, date-only fields in forms, YYYY-MM-DD parsing. Triggers: dates off by one day, timezone bugs, form date handling, competition dates, event dates, calendar dates.
- documentation: Centralizes Diataxis documentation framework guidance so Claude can write, review, or improve documentation following the four distinct types (tutorials, how-to guides, reference, explanation).
- drag-and-drop: Implement drag and drop using @atlaskit/pragmatic-drag-and-drop. Use when implementing sortable lists, reorderable items, kanban boards, or any drag-drop interactions. Covers draggable setup, drop targets, edge detection, drag previews, and critical state management patterns to avoid performance issues.
- e2e-test: End-to-end testing with Playwright. Use when writing E2E tests, implementing page objects, setting up test fixtures, handling authentication in tests, adding visual regression testing, or configuring E2E tests for CI. Covers when to use E2E vs integration tests, critical path identification, page object pattern, test isolation, and CI considerations.
- integration-test: Guide for writing integration tests with Vitest and Testing Library. Use when testing multi-component workflows, database interactions, React components with context providers, or full user flows. Covers the Testing Trophy philosophy (integration > unit), factory patterns for test data, MSW for network mocking, async testing patterns (waitFor, findBy), and custom render with providers. Use this for tests that cross multiple modules or layers of the application.
- local-db: Handle local database schema changes with Drizzle and PlanetScale. Use when making schema changes to src/db/schema.ts, adding/modifying database tables or columns, or when asked about database migrations. Covers the push-based local development workflow and when to generate migrations.
- logging: Observability logging patterns for server functions in TanStack Start on Cloudflare Workers. Use when: adding logging to server functions, debugging request flows, tracking entity CRUD operations, correlating logs across requests, adding observability to new features, troubleshooting request tracing, working with PostHog/OpenTelemetry logs. Triggers: logInfo, logError, logWarning, withRequestContext, addRequestContextAttribute, logEntityCreated, request tracing, observability, correlation IDs.
- my-pr-comments: Loads the pr review comments for the current branch.
- permissions: Guide for working with team-based permissions and authorization in the WODsmith codebase. Use when touching TEAM_PERMISSIONS constants, hasTeamPermission/requireTeamPermission functions, adding permission checks to actions or server functions, creating features requiring authorization, or ensuring client-server permission consistency.
- ralph-mode: Autonomous AI coding loop that ships features while you sleep. Use when the user wants to run Ralph, start an autonomous coding session, work overnight, or let Claude implement multiple user stories autonomously. Triggers on "ralph", "autonomous mode", "overnight coding", "autonomous loop", or requests to implement multiple stories without supervision.
- review: Centralizes persona-driven code reviews (Carmack, React core, Anders, React Router, Next.js, etc.) so Claude can pick or combine expert viewpoints when the user asks for a code review or perspective-specific critique. Use when user asks for code review, architecture feedback, or expert perspective on implementation.
- server-fn-auth: Authentication and authorization patterns for TanStack Start server functions.
Use when: adding auth to a new server function, fixing missing authorization,
checking user permissions, protecting endpoints, or implementing organizer/volunteer
access control for competitions.
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills using TypeScript/Bun scripts (Windows-compatible). Use when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Claude's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations.
- tanstack-start-boundaries: TanStack Start server/client code boundary patterns. Use when: debugging virtual module resolution errors (tanstack-start-injected-head-scripts, tanstack-start-manifest, #tanstack-router-entry), importing from @tanstack/react-start/server, using getCookie/getRequestHeaders/getRequestUrl, adding providers to router.tsx, or when Vite fails to resolve TanStack Start imports. Covers createServerFn patterns, dynamic imports, and proper placement of server-only code.
- tanstack-start-server-only: TanStack Start server-only patterns using createServerOnlyFn and cloudflare:workers env access.
Use when: accessing Cloudflare environment variables (APP_URL, STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, etc.),
creating server-only utility functions, working with Stripe or other server-only APIs,
or when you need to ensure code only runs on the server and throws if called from client.
Covers createServerOnlyFn vs createServerFn differences, env access patterns, and centralized
server-only utilities.
- team-memory: Store, search, and manage team memory from AI coding sessions. Use /remember to save observations (conventions, gotchas, debugging tips, architecture decisions, workflows). Use /recall to search memories by semantic query. Use /feedback to rate memory quality (helpful/harmful/irrelevant). Triggers on any request to save, recall, search, or rate team knowledge.
- test: Primary entry point for testing guidance. Routes to appropriate test type (unit, integration, E2E) based on what you're testing. Use when asked about "test", "testing", "write tests", "TDD", or test strategy. Embeds Testing Trophy philosophy and TDD Red-Green-Refactor as core discipline.
- type-safety: Fix type assertions and improve TypeScript type safety. Use when encountering 'as unknown as' casts, manual type definitions that duplicate schema types, or unclear type errors in database queries, especially with Drizzle ORM relations. Also use when verifying types
- unit-test: Guide for writing unit tests with Vitest. Use when writing tests for service functions, pure logic, or webhook handlers. Covers TDD Red-Green-Refactor cycle, Arrange-Act-Assert pattern, anti-patterns, pure function testing, and mocking at boundaries.
- create-pr: Create a PR from current changes with PRD gif
- fix-lint: /fix-lint
- linear-project: Work on a Linear project with structured workflow
- sync-diagrams: Store and synchronize digrams with codebase in ai/diagrams directory
- test-pr: Review PR changes and provide comprehensive test coverage recommendations using unit, integration, and e2e testing skills
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- autoresearch:autoresearch: Use when user types /autoresearch or asks to run an autonomous iteration loop against a goal/metric. Autonomous Goal-directed Iteration — modify, verify, keep/discard, repeat. Apply to ANY task with a measurable metric.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:debug: Use when user types /autoresearch:debug or asks to hunt bugs / find root cause iteratively. Autonomous bug-hunting loop — scientific method + autoresearch iteration. Finds ALL bugs, not just one.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:fix: Use when user types /autoresearch:fix or asks to repair errors iteratively until zero remain. Autonomous fix loop — one fix per iteration, atomic, auto-reverted on failure.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:learn: Use when user types /autoresearch:learn or asks to document/learn the codebase. Autonomous codebase documentation engine — scout, learn, generate/update docs with validation-fix loop.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:plan: Use when user types /autoresearch:plan or asks to turn a goal into Scope/Metric/Direction/Verify. Interactive wizard that builds the full autoresearch config from a single Goal.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:predict: Use when user types /autoresearch:predict or asks for multi-persona pre-analysis. Multi-persona swarm prediction — pre-analyze code from multiple expert perspectives using file-based knowledge representation. Zero external dependencies.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:reason: Use when user types /autoresearch:reason or asks for adversarial generate-critique-synthesize refinement. Isolated multi-agent adversarial refinement — generate, critique, synthesize, and judge outputs through repeated rounds until convergence. Produces a lineage of evolving candidates with documented decision rationale.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:scenario: Use when user types /autoresearch:scenario or asks to explore edge cases from a seed scenario. Scenario-driven use case generator — explores situations, edge cases, and derivative scenarios from a seed scenario using autonomous iteration.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:security: Use when user types /autoresearch:security or asks for STRIDE / OWASP / red-team audit. Autonomous security audit — STRIDE threat model + OWASP Top 10 + red-team with 4 adversarial personas.
- autoresearch:autoresearch:ship: Use when user types /autoresearch:ship or asks to run the 8-phase ship/deliver workflow. Universal shipping workflow — ship code, content, marketing, sales, research, or anything through a structured 8-phase workflow.
- swarm:handoff: Properly end a swarm session - release reservations, sync state, generate continuation prompt
- swarm:hive: Query and manage swarm tasks (cells)
- swarm:inbox: Check swarm mail inbox for messages from other agents
- swarm:status: Check swarm coordination status - workers, messages, reservations
- swarm:swarm: Decompose a task into parallel subtasks and coordinate execution
- autoresearch:autoresearch: Use when user types /autoresearch, /autoresearch:plan, /autoresearch:debug, /autoresearch:fix, /autoresearch:security, /autoresearch:ship, /autoresearch:scenario, /autoresearch:predict, /autoresearch:learn, or /autoresearch:reason, or mentions "autoresearch" with a goal/metric. Autonomous Goal-directed Iteration — apply Karpathy's autoresearch principles to ANY task: modify, verify, keep/discard, repeat. Supports bounded mode via Iterations: N inline config.
- swarm:always-on-guidance: Always-on rule-oriented guidance for claude-plugin agents. Use to align behavior, tool usage, and model-specific defaults (GPT-5.2-code vs Opus 4.5) while avoiding deprecated bd/cass references.
- swarm:swarm-coordination: Multi-agent coordination patterns for OpenCode swarm workflows. Use when work benefits from parallelization or coordination.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). 63	/** Override callbacks for cohost access to judge scheduling mutations */
64	export interface JudgeSchedulingOverrides {
65	  /** Override for rollbackToVersionFn */

67	    data: { versionId: string; teamId: string }
68	  }) => Promise<JudgeAssignmentVersion>

81	    data: Record<string, unknown>

120	interface JudgeSchedulingContainerProps {
121	  competitionId: string
122	  competitionSlug: string
123	  organizingTeamId: string
124	  competitionType: "in-person" | "online"
125	  events: CompetitionWorkout[]
126	  heats: HeatWithAssignments[]
127	  judges: JudgeVolunteerInfo[]
128	  judgeAssignments: JudgeHeatAssignment[]
129	  rotations: CompetitionJudgeRotation[]
130	  /** Map of event ID to event defaults */
131	  eventDefaultsMap: Map<string, EventDefaults>
132	  /** Map of event ID to version history */
133	  versionHistoryMap: Map<string, JudgeAssignmentVersion[]>
134	  /** Map of event ID to active version */
135	  activeVersionMap: Map<string, JudgeAssignmentVersion | null>
136	  /** Competition-level default heats per rotation */
137	  competitionDefaultHeats: number
138	  /** Competition-level default lane shift pattern */
139	  competitionDefaultPattern: LaneShiftPattern
140	  /** Currently selected event ID (from URL) */
141	  selectedEventId: string
142	  /** Callback when event selection changes */
143	  onEventChange: (eventId: string) => void
144	  /**
145	   * Hide the container's built-in event selector. Useful when the
146	   * parent page already renders one (e.g. the AI judge scheduling page
147	   * has its own selector that drives both the agent and this editor).
148	   */
149	  hideEventSelector?: boolean
150	  /**
151	   * Optional slot rendered above the Rotations section. The AI
152	   * scheduling page uses this for the activity log narration that
153	   * appears as the agent works.
154	   */
155	  rotationsHeaderSlot?: React.ReactNode
156	  /**
157	   * Optional slot rendered next to the Publish button in the rotation
158	   * overview row. The AI scheduling page uses this for the
159	   * "Generate AI suggestions" button so it sits next to Publish (the
160	   * primary CTA) instead of floating above the section.
161	   */
162	  rotationOverviewActionsSlot?: React.ReactNode
163	  /** Optional override callbacks for cohost routes */
164	  overrides?: JudgeSchedulingOverrides
165	}

167	/**
168	 * Main container for judge scheduling.
169	 * Shows event selector, overview, available judges panel, and heat cards.
170	 */
171	export function JudgeSchedulingContainer({
172	  competitionId,
173	  competitionSlug,
174	  organizingTeamId,
175	  competitionType,
176	  events,
177	  heats,
178	  judges,
179	  judgeAssignments: initialAssignments,
180	  rotations: initialRotations,
181	  eventDefaultsMap,
182	  versionHistoryMap,
183	  activeVersionMap,
184	  competitionDefaultHeats,
185	  competitionDefaultPattern,
186	  selectedEventId,
187	  onEventChange,
188	  hideEventSelector = false,
189	  rotationsHeaderSlot,
190	  rotationOverviewActionsSlot,
191	  overrides,
192	}: JudgeSchedulingContainerProps) {
193	  const navigate = useNavigate()
194	  const hasHeatScheduling = canUseHeatScheduling(competitionType)
195	  const [assignments, setAssignments] =
196	    useState<JudgeHeatAssignment[]>(initialAssignments)
197	  const [selectedJudgeIds, setSelectedJudgeIds] = useState<Set<string>>(
198	    new Set(),
199	  )
200	  const [selectedVersionId, setSelectedVersionId] = useState<string | null>(
201	    null,
202	  )
203	  const [isRollingBack, setIsRollingBack] = useState(false)
204	  const [isFetchingAssignments, setIsFetchingAssignments] = useState(false)
205	  const [filterEmptyLanes, setFilterEmptyLanes] = useState(true)
206	  const [availableJudgeSearch, setAvailableJudgeSearch] = useState("")
207	
208	  // Get heats for selected event
209	  const eventHeats = useMemo(
210	    () =>
211	      heats
212	        .filter((h) => h.trackWorkoutId === selectedEventId)
213	        .sort((a, b) => a.heatNumber - b.heatNumber),
214	    [heats, selectedEventId],
215	  )
216	
217	  // Get max lanes from venue or default to 10
218	  const maxLanes = useMemo(() => {
219	    const venueWithLanes = eventHeats.find((h) => h.venue?.laneCount)
220	    return venueWithLanes?.venue?.laneCount ?? 10
221	  }, [eventHeats])
222	
223	  // Get assigned judge IDs for current event
224	  const assignedJudgeIds = useMemo(() => {
225	    const eventHeatIds = new Set(eventHeats.map((h) => h.id))
226	    return new Set(
227	      assignments
228	        .filter((a) => eventHeatIds.has(a.heatId))
229	        .map((a) => a.membershipId),
230	    )
231	  }, [eventHeats, assignments])
232	
233	  // Get unassigned judges for current event
234	  const unassignedJudges = useMemo(
235	    () => judges.filter((j) => !assignedJudgeIds.has(j.membershipId)),
236	    [judges, assignedJudgeIds],
237	  )
238	
239	  // Get all judges sorted by total heat assignment count (ascending - least assigned first)
240	  const judgesByAssignmentCount = useMemo(() => {
241	    // Count assignments per judge across ALL heats (not just current event)
242	    const assignmentCounts = new Map<string, number>()
243	    for (const judge of judges) {
244	      assignmentCounts.set(judge.membershipId, 0)
245	    }
246	    for (const assignment of assignments) {
247	      const membershipId = assignment.membershipId
248	      if (!membershipId) continue
249	      const count = assignmentCounts.get(membershipId) ?? 0
250	      assignmentCounts.set(membershipId, count + 1)
251	    }
252	
253	    // Sort judges by assignment count (ascending)
254	    return [...judges]
255	      .map((judge) => ({
256	        ...judge,
257	        assignmentCount: assignmentCounts.get(judge.membershipId) ?? 0,
258	      }))
259	      .sort((a, b) => a.assignmentCount - b.assignmentCount)
260	  }, [judges, assignments])
261	
262	  // Filter judges by search query
263	  const filteredJudgesByAssignmentCount = useMemo(() => {
264	    if (!availableJudgeSearch.trim()) return judgesByAssignmentCount
265	    const query = availableJudgeSearch.toLowerCase().trim()
266	    return judgesByAssignmentCount.filter((judge) => {
267	      const fullName =
268	        `${judge.firstName ?? ""} ${judge.lastName ?? ""}`.toLowerCase()
269	      return fullName.includes(query)
270	    })
271	  }, [judgesByAssignmentCount, availableJudgeSearch])
272	
273	  // Get rotations for selected event - track locally to update on changes
274	  const initialEventRotations = useMemo(
275	    () => initialRotations.filter((r) => r.trackWorkoutId === selectedEventId),
276	    [initialRotations, selectedEventId],
277	  )
278	  const [eventRotations, setEventRotations] = useState(initialEventRotations)
279	
280	  // Sync when event changes or initial data changes
281	  useEffect(() => {
282	    setEventRotations(initialEventRotations)
283	  }, [initialEventRotations])
284	
285	  // Get selected event details
286	  const selectedEvent = useMemo(
287	    () => events.find((e) => e.id === selectedEventId),
288	    [events, selectedEventId],
289	  )
290	
291	  // Get version data for selected event - track locally to update on publish
292	  const initialVersionHistory = useMemo(
293	    () => versionHistoryMap.get(selectedEventId) ?? [],
294	    [versionHistoryMap, selectedEventId],
295	  )
296	  const initialActiveVersion = useMemo(
297	    () => activeVersionMap.get(selectedEventId) ?? null,
298	    [activeVersionMap, selectedEventId],
299	  )
300	  const [eventVersionHistory, setEventVersionHistory] = useState(
301	    initialVersionHistory,
302	  )
303	  const [eventActiveVersion, setEventActiveVersion] =
304	    useState(initialActiveVersion)
305	
306	  // Sync when event changes
307	  useEffect(() => {
308	    setEventVersionHistory(initialVersionHistory)
309	    setEventActiveVersion(initialActiveVersion)
310	  }, [initialVersionHistory, initialActiveVersion])
311	
312	  // Auto-select active version when event changes
313	  useEffect(() => {
314	    if (eventActiveVersion) {
315	      setSelectedVersionId(eventActiveVersion.id)
316	    } else {
317	      setSelectedVersionId(null)
318	    }
319	  }, [eventActiveVersion])
320	
321	  // Refresh version data after publishing
322	  async function refreshVersionData() {
323	    try {
324	      const [historyResult, activeResult] = await Promise.all([
325	        getVersionHistoryFn({ data: { trackWorkoutId: selectedEventId } }),
326	        getActiveVersionFn({ data: { trackWorkoutId: selectedEventId } }),
327	      ])
328	      if (historyResult) {
329	        setEventVersionHistory(historyResult)
330	      }
331	      setEventActiveVersion(activeResult)
332	
333	      // Also fetch assignments for the new active version
334	      if (activeResult) {
335	        setSelectedVersionId(activeResult.id)
336	        const assignmentsResult = await getJudgeHeatAssignmentsFn({
337	          data: { trackWorkoutId: selectedEventId, versionId: activeResult.id },
338	        })
339	        if (assignmentsResult) {
340	          setAssignments((prev) => {
341	            // Remove old assignments for this event, add new ones
342	            const eventHeatIds = new Set(eventHeats.map((h) => h.id))
343	            const withoutEvent = prev.filter((a) => !eventHeatIds.has(a.heatId))
344	            return [...withoutEvent, ...assignmentsResult]
345	          })
346	        }
347	      }
348	    } catch (err) {
349	      console.error("Failed to refresh version data:", err)
350	    }
351	  }
352	
353	  // Handle version change
354	  async function handleVersionChange(versionId: string) {
355	    if (versionId === eventActiveVersion?.id) {
356	      // Already active, just update UI
357	      setSelectedVersionId(versionId)
358	      return
359	    }
360	
361	    // Rollback to different version
362	    setIsRollingBack(true)
363	    try {
364	      const rollbackFn = overrides?.rollbackToVersion ?? rollbackToVersionFn
365	      const result = await rollbackFn({
366	        data: {
367	          teamId: organizingTeamId,
368	          versionId,
369	        },
370	      })
371	
372	      if (result) {
373	        toast.success("Version activated successfully")
374	        setSelectedVersionId(versionId)
375	
376	        // Fetch assignments for the new version
377	        setIsFetchingAssignments(true)
378	        try {
379	          const assignmentsResult = await getJudgeHeatAssignmentsFn({
380	            data: { trackWorkoutId: selectedEventId, versionId },
381	          })
382	          if (assignmentsResult) {
383	            setAssignments((prev) => {
384	              // Remove old assignments for this event, add new ones
385	              const eventHeatIds = new Set(eventHeats.map((h) => h.id))
386	              const withoutEvent = prev.filter(
387	                (a) => !eventHeatIds.has(a.heatId),
388	              )
389	              return [...withoutEvent, ...assignmentsResult]
390	            })
391	          }
392	        } finally {
393	          setIsFetchingAssignments(false)
394	        }
395	      }
396	    } catch (_err) {
397	      toast.error("Failed to activate version")
398	    } finally {
399	      setIsRollingBack(false)
400	    }
401	  }
402	
403	  // Get event defaults for selected event (with fallback to competition defaults)
404	  const selectedEventDefaults = useMemo(() => {
405	    const eventDefaults = eventDefaultsMap.get(selectedEventId)
406	    return {
407	      defaultHeatsCount:
408	        eventDefaults?.defaultHeatsCount ?? competitionDefaultHeats,
409	      defaultLaneShiftPattern:
410	        eventDefaults?.defaultLaneShiftPattern ?? competitionDefaultPattern,
411	      minHeatBuffer: eventDefaults?.minHeatBuffer ?? 2,
412	      // Raw values (null if not overridden at event level)
413	      rawDefaultHeatsCount: eventDefaults?.defaultHeatsCount ?? null,
414	      rawDefaultLaneShiftPattern:
415	        eventDefaults?.defaultLaneShiftPattern ?? null,
416	      rawMinHeatBuffer: eventDefaults?.minHeatBuffer ?? null,
417	    }
418	  }, [
419	    selectedEventId,
420	    eventDefaultsMap,
421	    competitionDefaultHeats,
422	    competitionDefaultPattern,
423	  ])
424	
425	  // Compute occupied lanes per heat from athlete assignments
426	  const occupiedLanesByHeat = useMemo(() => {
427	    const map = new Map<number, Set<number>>()
428	    for (const heat of eventHeats) {
429	      const occupiedLanes = new Set<number>()
430	      for (const assignment of heat.assignments) {
431	        occupiedLanes.add(assignment.laneNumber)
432	      }
433	      map.set(heat.heatNumber, occupiedLanes)
434	    }
435	    return map
436	  }, [eventHeats])
437	
438	  // Calculate rotation coverage for selected event
439	  const rotationCoverage = useMemo(() => {
440	    if (eventHeats.length === 0) {
441	      return {
442	        totalSlots: 0,
443	        coveredSlots: 0,
444	        coveragePercent: 0,
445	        gaps: [],
446	        overlaps: [],
447	      }
448	    }
449	
450	    const heatsData = eventHeats.map((h) => {
451	      const base = {
452	        heatNumber: h.heatNumber,
453	        laneCount: h.venue?.laneCount ?? maxLanes,
454	      }
455	      // Include occupiedLanes if filtering is enabled
456	      if (filterEmptyLanes) {
457	        // Compute occupied lanes directly from heat assignments to ensure consistency
458	        const occupiedLanes = new Set<number>()
459	        for (const assignment of h.assignments) {
460	          occupiedLanes.add(assignment.laneNumber)
461	        }
462	        return {
463	          ...base,
464	          occupiedLanes,
465	        }
466	      }
467	      return base
468	    })
469	
470	    return calculateCoverage(eventRotations, heatsData)
471	  }, [eventRotations, eventHeats, maxLanes, filterEmptyLanes])
472	
473	  // Handle multi-select toggle
474	  function handleToggleSelect(membershipId: string, shiftKey: boolean) {
475	    setSelectedJudgeIds((prev) => {
476	      const next = new Set(prev)
477	      if (shiftKey && prev.size > 0) {
478	        // Range select - find indices using the sorted list
479	        const sortedJudges = judgesByAssignmentCount
480	        const lastSelected = Array.from(prev).pop()
481	        const lastIndex = sortedJudges.findIndex(
482	          (j) => j.membershipId === lastSelected,
483	        )
484	        const currentIndex = sortedJudges.findIndex(
485	          (j) => j.membershipId === membershipId,
486	        )
487	        if (lastIndex !== -1 && currentIndex !== -1) {
488	          const start = Math.min(lastIndex, currentIndex)
489	          const end = Math.max(lastIndex, currentIndex)
490	          for (let i = start; i <= end; i++) {
491	            const judge = sortedJudges[i]
492	            if (judge) {
493	              next.add(judge.membershipId)
494	            }
495	          }
496	          return next
497	        }
498	      }
499	      // Toggle single
500	      if (next.has(membershipId)) {
501	        next.delete(membershipId)
502	      } else {
503	        next.add(membershipId)
504	      }
505	      return next
506	    })
507	  }
508	
509	  function clearSelection() {
510	    setSelectedJudgeIds(new Set())
511	  }
512	
513	  // Handle assignment changes from heat cards
514	  function handleAssignmentChange(
515	    heatId: string,
516	    newAssignments: JudgeHeatAssignment[],
517	  ) {
518	    setAssignments((prev) => {
519	      // Remove old assignments for this heat, add new ones
520	      const withoutHeat = prev.filter((a) => a.heatId !== heatId)
521	      return [...withoutHeat, ...newAssignments]
522	    })
523	  }
524	
525	  // Handle cross-heat moves
526	  function handleMoveAssignment(
527	    assignmentId: string,
528	    _sourceHeatId: string,
529	    targetHeatId: string,
530	    targetLane: number,

content.content:
[[lat.md/crew#Crew#Judge Rotations]] (lat.md/crew.md:237-248)

> ## Judge Rotations
>
> Crew judge rotations adapt the existing judge scheduling model into a local-operator event surface.
>
> [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/judges.tsx]] selects a workout and renders the drag-and-drop judging grid plus publishing described in [[crew#Judge Rotations#Judge Assignments Grid]].
>
> [[apps/crew/src/server-fns/crew-judge-rotations-fns.ts]] keeps route imports lightweight while [[apps/crew/src/server/crew-judge-rotations.server.ts]] owns Crew event hydration, scoped rotation writes, and publishing through the versioned assignment helper.
>
> [[apps/crew/src/lib/crew/judge-rotations.ts]] keeps rotation expansion, validation, lane conflicts, and coverage summaries deterministic for the route and server.
>
> Publishing rotations creates a new active `judge_assignment_versions` row and materializes `judge_heat_assignments` under the advisory-lock flow from [[crew#Judge Assignment Version Publishing]].
>
> ### Judge Roster Eligibility and Imported Judges
>
> The judge roster includes every staffable volunteer on the competition team, whether account-backed or invitation-based, that is judge-eligible.
>
> `loadCrewJudgeVolunteers` builds the roster from the same membership + invitation union as [[crew#Roster Shifts Assignments]] ([[apps/crew/src/server/crew-roster-shift.server.ts#loadCrewRoster]]), so imported / manually-added volunteers (stored as `team_invitations` with no user account) appear alongside account-backed memberships. Eligibility is decided by [[apps/crew/src/lib/crew/judge-rotations.ts#isCrewJudgeEligible]], which accepts `judge` / `head_judge` AND `general` — mirroring how General acts as a shift wildcard ([[apps/crew/src/lib/crew/roster-shifts.ts#isVolunteerCompatibleWithShift]]). Organizers routinely import a batch of General volunteers intending to seat them as judges, so excluding General would leave the grid empty after a bulk import.
>
> Every judge is keyed by a canonical assignee id: the membership id (`tmem_`) for account-backed volunteers, or the invitation id (`tinv_`) for imported ones. `CrewJudgeVolunteer.membershipId` carries this canonical id so the grid keys judges unchanged. `competition_judge_rotations` and `judge_heat_assignments` each carry a nullable `membershipId` and a nullable `invitationId` (exactly one set), parallel to `volunteer_shift_assignments`. Saving, conflict validation, materialization, and active-assignment hydration all resolve through the canonical id, so invitation-based judges are assignable and publishable end to end. Day-of check-in / replacement remains membership-keyed and rejects accountless judges with a clear message until that flow is widened.
>
> ### Judge Assignments Grid
>
> The Crew judging grid is the wodsmith-start organizer judging experience ported into Crew: judges are dragged from a roster panel onto heat lanes, with multi-select bulk drops and cross-heat moves.
>
> [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-scheduling-container.tsx]] holds the grid layout (workout selector, available-judges panel, heat cards, overview, publish panel) and the local cell state. [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-heat-card.tsx]] and [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/draggable-judge.tsx]] provide the `@atlaskit/pragmatic-drag-and-drop` draggable judges and droppable lanes.
>
> The grid reconciles wodsmith-start's per-heat judge placement with Crew's per-volunteer rotation model: every grid cell (one judge in one heat + lane) is a single-heat rotation (`heatsCount = 1`, lane-shift `stay`). [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-grid-utils.ts]] converts saved rotations to grid cells and back ([[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-grid-utils.ts#rotationsToGridCells]], [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-grid-utils.ts#gridCellsToRotationRows]]). Each drop, move, or remove persists the affected judge's full cell set through `saveCrewJudgeRotationsForVolunteerFn`, and the Publish button materializes them with `publishCrewJudgeRotationsFn` — no new server functions are added.
>

[[lat.md/crew#Crew#Judge Rotations]] (lat.md/crew.md:237-248)

> ## Judge Rotations
>
> Crew judge rotations adapt the existing judge scheduling model into a local-operator event surface.
>
> [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/judges.tsx]] selects a workout and renders the drag-and-drop judging grid plus publishing described in [[crew#Judge Rotations#Judge Assignments Grid]].
>
> [[apps/crew/src/server-fns/crew-judge-rotations-fns.ts]] keeps route imports lightweight while [[apps/crew/src/server/crew-judge-rotations.server.ts]] owns Crew event hydration, scoped rotation writes, and publishing through the versioned assignment helper.
>
> [[apps/crew/src/lib/crew/judge-rotations.ts]] keeps rotation expansion, validation, lane conflicts, and coverage summaries deterministic for the route and server.
>
> Publishing rotations creates a new active `judge_assignment_versions` row and materializes `judge_heat_assignments` under the advisory-lock flow from [[crew#Judge Assignment Version Publishing]].
>
> ### Judge Roster Eligibility and Imported Judges
>
> The judge roster includes every staffable volunteer on the competition team, whether account-backed or invitation-based, that is judge-eligible.
>
> `loadCrewJudgeVolunteers` builds the roster from the same membership + invitation union as [[crew#Roster Shifts Assignments]] ([[apps/crew/src/server/crew-roster-shift.server.ts#loadCrewRoster]]), so imported / manually-added volunteers (stored as `team_invitations` with no user account) appear alongside account-backed memberships. Eligibility is decided by [[apps/crew/src/lib/crew/judge-rotations.ts#isCrewJudgeEligible]], which accepts `judge` / `head_judge` AND `general` — mirroring how General acts as a shift wildcard ([[apps/crew/src/lib/crew/roster-shifts.ts#isVolunteerCompatibleWithShift]]). Organizers routinely import a batch of General volunteers intending to seat them as judges, so excluding General would leave the grid empty after a bulk import.
>
> Every judge is keyed by a canonical assignee id: the membership id (`tmem_`) for account-backed volunteers, or the invitation id (`tinv_`) for imported ones. `CrewJudgeVolunteer.membershipId` carries this canonical id so the grid keys judges unchanged. `competition_judge_rotations` and `judge_heat_assignments` each carry a nullable `membershipId` and a nullable `invitationId` (exactly one set), parallel to `volunteer_shift_assignments`. Saving, conflict validation, materialization, and active-assignment hydration all resolve through the canonical id, so invitation-based judges are assignable and publishable end to end. Day-of check-in / replacement remains membership-keyed and rejects accountless judges with a clear message until that flow is widened.
>
> ### Judge Assignments Grid
>
> The Crew judging grid is the wodsmith-start organizer judging experience ported into Crew: judges are dragged from a roster panel onto heat lanes, with multi-select bulk drops and cross-heat moves.
>
> [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-scheduling-container.tsx]] holds the grid layout (workout selector, available-judges panel, heat cards, overview, publish panel) and the local cell state. [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-heat-card.tsx]] and [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/draggable-judge.tsx]] provide the `@atlaskit/pragmatic-drag-and-drop` draggable judges and droppable lanes.
>
> The grid reconciles wodsmith-start's per-heat judge placement with Crew's per-volunteer rotation model: every grid cell (one judge in one heat + lane) is a single-heat rotation (`heatsCount = 1`, lane-shift `stay`). [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-grid-utils.ts]] converts saved rotations to grid cells and back ([[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-grid-utils.ts#rotationsToGridCells]], [[apps/crew/src/routes/events/$eventId/-components/judges/judge-grid-utils.ts#gridCellsToRotationRows]]). Each drop, move, or remove persists the affected judge's full cell set through `saveCrewJudgeRotationsForVolunteerFn`, and the Publish button materializes them with `publishCrewJudgeRotationsFn` — no new server functions are added.
>

Grid 57 * placements are kept as local cells and persisted per judge as single-heat 58 * rotations; publishing materializes them through Crew's existing publish path. 59 * 60 * Built for FAST fill: drag a judge onto a lane, or pick an "active judge" and 61 * click lanes to seat them, plus one-click bulk actions (fill a heat, seat a 62 * judge down a whole lane across the workout, auto-distribute the whole grid). 63 * Crew has no athlete/lane-occupancy data, so every lane of every heat is 64 * always droppable and visible. 65 */ 66 export function JudgeSchedulingContainer({ 67 eventId, 68 page, 69 selectedWorkoutId, 70 onWorkoutChange, 71 }: JudgeSchedulingContainerProps) { 72 const router = useRouter() 73 const saveRotations = useServerFn(saveCrewJudgeRotationsForVolunteerFn) 74 const publishRotations = useServerFn(publishCrewJudgeRotationsFn) 75 76 const { workouts, heats, judges } = page 77 78 const [availableJudgeSearch, setAvailableJudgeSearch] = useState("") 79 const [selectedJudgeIds, setSelectedJudgeIds] = useState>( 80 new Set(), 81 ) 82 const [publishNotes, setPublishNotes] = useState("") 83 const [isPublishing, setIsPublishing] = useState(false) 84 const [pendingJudgeIds, setPendingJudgeIds] = useState>(new Set()) 85 86 // Heats for the selected workout, sorted by heat number. 87 const workoutHeats = useMemo( 88 () => 89 heats 90 .filter((h) => h.trackWorkoutId === selectedWorkoutId) 91 .sort((a, b) => a.heatNumber - b.heatNumber), 92 [heats, selectedWorkoutId], 93 ) 94 95 // Max lanes across this workout's heats (fallback 10). Lane count comes from 96 // each heat's location/venue, never from athletes. 97 const maxLanes = useMemo(() => { 98 const max = workoutHeats.reduce( 99 (acc, heat) => Math.max(acc, heat.laneCount), 100 0, 101 ) 102 return max > 0 ? max : 10 103 }, [workoutHeats]) 104 105 // Saved rotations for the selected workout, expanded into grid cells. 106 const savedGridCells = useMemo(() => { 107 const workoutRotations = page.rotations.filter( 108 (rotation) => rotation.trackWorkoutId === selectedWorkoutId, 109 ) 110 return rotationsToGridCells(workoutRotations, workoutHeats) 111 }, [page.rotations, selectedWorkoutId, workoutHeats]) 112 113 // Local, editable grid state. [] 130 list.push(cell) 131 map.set(cell.heatNumber, list) 132 } 133 return map 134 }, [gridCells]) 135 136 // Judges assigned somewhere in the current workout. 137 const assignedJudgeIds = useMemo( 138 () => new Set(gridCells.map((cell) => cell.membershipId)), 139 [gridCells], 140 ) 141 142 const unassignedJudges = useMemo( 143 () => judges.filter((j) => !assignedJudgeIds.has(j.membershipId)), 144 [judges, assignedJudgeIds], 145 ) 146 147 // All judges sorted by assignment count (ascending — least assigned first). 148 const judgesByAssignmentCount = useMemo(() => { 149 const counts = new Map() 150 for (const judge of judges) counts.set(judge.membershipId, 0) 151 for (const cell of gridCells) { 152 counts.set(cell.membershipId, (counts.get(cell.membershipId) ?? 0) + 1) 153 } 154 return [...judges] 155 .map((judge) => ({ 156 ...judge, 157 assignmentCount: counts.get(judge.membershipId) ?? 0, 158 })) 159 .sort((a, b) => a.assignmentCount - b.assignmentCount) 160 }, [judges, gridCells]) 161 162 const filteredJudgesByAssignmentCount = useMemo(() => { 163 if (!availableJudgeSearch.trim()) return judgesByAssignmentCount 164 const query = availableJudgeSearch.toLowerCase().trim() 165 return judgesByAssignmentCount.filter((judge) => 166 getCrewJudgeName(judge).toLowerCase().includes(query), 167 ) 168 }, [judgesByAssignmentCount, availableJudgeSearch]) 169 170 const selectedWorkout = workouts.find( 171 (workout) => workout.id === selectedWorkoutId, 172 ) 173 const activeVersion = selectedWorkoutId 174 ? page.activeVersionByWorkout[selectedWorkoutId] 175 : null 176 177 // "Active judge" click-to-assign mode: when exactly one judge is selected, 178 // clicking lanes seats/removes that judge — faster than dragging for filling 179 // many cells. 180 const activeJudgeId = 181 selectedJudgeIds.size === 1 ? laneNumber && 255 cell.membershipId !== membershipId, 256 ) 257 next.push({ membershipId, heatNumber, laneNumber }) 258 void persistJudges( 259 [membershipId, ...displaced.map((cell) => aiTitle: Add judge scheduling grid to crew app

content.text: Now I understand the structure. wodsmith-start has two sections: **Published Assignments** (heat-lane grid, Image #1, gated by active version) and **Rotations** (RotationOverview + RotationTimeline grid, Image #2, always shown). Crew only has the heat-lane grid and is missing the Rotation Timeline grid. Let me read the missing components.

363	  // Build maps between display index (1-based) and actual heat number

390	    >()

392	    // Initialize all slots using display indices but looking up by actual heat number
393	    for (let displayIdx = 1; displayIdx <= heatsCount; displayIdx++) {
394	      const actualHeatNumber = displayToHeatNumber.get(displayIdx) ?? displayIdx

420	        // Convert actual heat number to display index for grid lookup
421	        const displayIdx = heatNumberToDisplay.get(assignment.heatNumber)
422	        if (displayIdx === undefined) continue
423	        const key = `${displayIdx}:${assignment.laneNumber}`
424	        const current = grid.get(key)
425	        if (!current) continue
426	
427	        if (current.rotationIds.length > 0) {
428	          grid.set(key, {
429	            status: "overlap",
430	            rotationIds: [...current.rotationIds, rotation.id],
431	          })
432	        } else {
433	          grid.set(key, { status: "covered", rotationIds: [rotation.id] })
434	        }
435	      }
436	    }
437	
438	    // When a volunteer is being edited/selected, mark buffer zones
439	    if (editingVolunteerId) {
440	      const volunteerRotations = rotationsByVolunteer.get(editingVolunteerId)
441	      if (volunteerRotations) {
442	        for (const rotation of volunteerRotations) {
443	          const assignments = expandRotationToAssignments(rotation, heats, {
444	            respectOccupiedLanes: filterEmptyLanes,
445	          })
446	          if (assignments.length === 0) continue
447	
448	          // Get the heat range of this rotation (using display indices)
449	          const displayIndices = assignments
450	            .map((a) => heatNumberToDisplay.get(a.heatNumber))
451	            .filter((idx): idx is number => idx !== undefined)
452	          if (displayIndices.length === 0) continue
453	          const rotationStart = Math.min(...displayIndices)
454	          const rotationEnd = Math.max(...displayIndices)
455	
456	          // Calculate buffer zones (in display indices)
457	          // Buffer after: (rotationEnd, rotationEnd + minHeatBuffer]
458	          const bufferAfterEnd = rotationEnd + minHeatBuffer
459	
460	          // Buffer before: [rotationStart - minHeatBuffer, rotationStart)
461	          const bufferBeforeStart = rotationStart - minHeatBuffer
462	
463	          // Mark buffer zone cells (all lanes in buffer heats)
464	          for (
465	            let displayIdx = bufferBeforeStart;
466	            displayIdx <= bufferAfterEnd;
467	            displayIdx++
468	          ) {
469	            // Skip heats within the rotation itself
470	            if (displayIdx >= rotationStart && displayIdx <= rotationEnd)
471	              continue
472	            // Skip heats outside valid range
473	            if (displayIdx < 1 || displayIdx > heatsCount) continue
474	
475	            for (let lane = 1; lane <= laneCount; lane++) {
476	              const key = `${displayIdx}:${lane}`
477	              const current = grid.get(key)
478	              if (!current) continue
479	
480	              // Only mark as buffer-blocked if not already covered/overlapped
481	              // (we want to show that existing assignments would be conflicts)
482	              if (current.status === "empty") {
483	                grid.set(key, {
484	                  status: "buffer-blocked",
485	                  rotationIds: current.rotationIds,
486	                })
487	              }
488	            }
489	          }
490	        }
491	      }
492	    }
493	
494	    return grid
495	  }, [
496	    rotations,
497	    heats,
498	    heatsCount,
499	    laneCount,
500	    editingVolunteerId,
501	    rotationsByVolunteer,
502	    minHeatBuffer,
503	    filterEmptyLanes,

1439	interface TimelineCellProps {
1440	  heat: number
1441	  lane: number
1442	  status: "empty" | "covered" | "overlap" | "buffer-blocked" | "unavailable"
1443	  isHighlighted: boolean
1444	  isPreview: boolean
1445	  /** Block index for preview cells (used for coloring) */
1446	  previewBlockIndex?: number
1447	  /** Color palette for blocks */
1448	  blockColors: Array<{ bg: string; border: string }>
1449	  /** True if this preview cell conflicts with an existing assignment */
1450	  isPreviewConflict: boolean
1451	  /** True if this cell is part of the rotations currently being edited */
1452	  isEditingCell: boolean
1453	  /** Minimum heat buffer (for tooltip) */
1454	  minHeatBuffer: number
1455	  onClick: () => void
1456	  /** Handler for when a rotation is dropped on this cell */
1457	  onRotationDrop: (rotation: CompetitionJudgeRotation) => void
1458	}
1459	
1460	function TimelineCell({
1461	  heat: _heat,
1462	  lane: _lane,
1463	  status,
1464	  isHighlighted,
1465	  isPreview,
1466	  previewBlockIndex,
1467	  blockColors,
1468	  isPreviewConflict,
1469	  isEditingCell,
1470	  minHeatBuffer,
1471	  onClick,
1472	  onRotationDrop,
1473	}: TimelineCellProps) {
1474	  const ref = useRef<HTMLButtonElement>(null)
1475	  const [isDraggedOver, setIsDraggedOver] = useState(false)
1476	
1477	  // Skip drop target registration and interaction for unavailable cells
1478	  const isUnavailable = status === "unavailable"
1479	
1480	  useEffect(() => {
1481	    if (isUnavailable) return
1482	
1483	    const element = ref.current
1484	    if (!element) return
1485	
1486	    return dropTargetForElements({
1487	      element,
1488	      canDrop: ({ source }) => source.data.type === "rotation",
1489	      onDragEnter: () => setIsDraggedOver(true),
1490	      onDragLeave: () => setIsDraggedOver(false),
1491	      onDrop: ({ source }) => {
1492	        setIsDraggedOver(false)
1493	        const rotation = source.data.rotation as
1494	          | CompetitionJudgeRotation
1495	          | undefined
1496	        if (rotation) {
1497	          onRotationDrop(rotation)
1498	        }
1499	      },
1500	    })
1501	  }, [onRotationDrop, isUnavailable])
1502	
1503	  // Determine background class based on status, highlight, preview, and editing state
1504	  let bgClass: string
1505	  let title: string | undefined
1506	
1507	  if (isUnavailable) {
1508	    // Unavailable cell - dark with diagonal stripes, non-interactive
1509	    bgClass =
1510	      "bg-neutral-200 dark:bg-neutral-800 bg-[repeating-linear-gradient(45deg,transparent,transparent_4px,rgba(0,0,0,0.1)_4px,rgba(0,0,0,0.1)_8px)] dark:bg-[repeating-linear-gradient(45deg,transparent,transparent_4px,rgba(255,255,255,0.1)_4px,rgba(255,255,255,0.1)_8px)] cursor-not-allowed"
1511	    title = "No athlete assigned"
1512	  } else if (isPreviewConflict) {
1513	    // Preview cell that conflicts with existing assignment - RED
1514	    bgClass = "bg-red-500/40 border-dashed border-2 border-red-500"
1515	    title = "Conflict: Heat already assigned"
1516	  } else if (isPreview && previewBlockIndex !== undefined) {
1517	    // Preview cells without conflict - use block-specific color
1518	    const colors = blockColors[previewBlockIndex % blockColors.length] ?? {
1519	      bg: "bg-blue-500/30",
1520	      border: "border-blue-500",
1521	    }
1522	    bgClass = `${colors.bg} border-dashed border-2 ${colors.border}`
1523	    title = `Preview: Block ${previewBlockIndex + 1}`
1524	  } else if (isEditingCell) {
1525	    // Cells currently covered by the rotations being edited - outline style (no fill)
1526	    bgClass = "bg-background ring-2 ring-inset ring-emerald-500"
1527	    title = "Current assignment"
1528	  } else if (isHighlighted) {
1529	    bgClass = "bg-primary/50 ring-2 ring-inset ring-primary"
1530	    title = "Selected rotation"
1531	  } else if (status === "buffer-blocked") {
1532	    // Buffer zone - amber with diagonal stripes pattern
1533	    bgClass = "bg-amber-500/20 border-amber-500/40 border-2"
1534	    title = `Buffer zone: Requires ${minHeatBuffer} heat gap between rotations`
1535	  } else if (status === "empty") {
1536	    bgClass = "bg-background hover:bg-muted/50"
1537	  } else if (status === "covered") {
1538	    bgClass = "bg-emerald-500/30"
1539	    title = "Covered"
1540	  } else {
1541	    bgClass = "bg-amber-500/30"
1542	    title = "Overlap: Multiple judges assigned"
1543	  }
1544	
1545	  return (
1546	    <button
1547	      ref={ref}
1548	      type="button"
1549	      onClick={isUnavailable ? undefined : onClick}
1550	      title={title}
1551	      disabled={isUnavailable}
1552	      className={`border-b border-r transition-colors last:border-r-0 ${bgClass} ${
1553	        isDraggedOver ? "ring-2 ring-inset ring-blue-500" : ""
1554	      } ${isUnavailable ? "" : "cursor-pointer"}`}
1555	    />
1556	  )
1557	}

17	const eventIdSchema = z.string().min(1, "Event ID is required")
18	const trackWorkoutIdSchema = z.string().min(1, "Workout ID is required")
19	// The grid keys judges by a canonical assignee id: a membership id (tmem_) for

21	// volunteers without an account.
22	const assigneeIdSchema = z
23	  .string()

29	  LANE_SHIFT_PATTERN.STAY,

36	})

41	  membershipId: assigneeIdSchema,
42	  laneShiftPattern: laneShiftPatternSchema,

44	    .array(

50	      }),

55	const publishCrewJudgeRotationsInputSchema = z.object({
56	  eventId: eventIdSchema,
57	  trackWorkoutId: trackWorkoutIdSchema,
58	  notes: optionalNotesSchema,
59	})

62	  .inputValidator((data: unknown) =>

76	    saveCrewJudgeRotationsForVolunteerInputSchema.parse(data),
